# UC-21.2.17 · Inventory Demand-Supply Mismatch Detection

## Description

For each material, the detection joins the latest inventory snapshot to rolling demand averages, computes days-of-cover versus replenishment lead time, labels rows as imminent stock-out risk or excess stocking, and returns only materials that deviate from a healthy coverage band.

## Value

Planners protect revenue and service levels by adjusting purchase orders and production schedules before a line-down event, while also releasing cash tied up in slow-moving overstocks instead of discovering the problem weeks later in a static spreadsheet.

## Implementation

Prefer a scheduled summary of `forecast:demand` by `material_id` to shrink the subsearch. Align `lead_time_days` to the active vendor route; buffer factors (here 1.2 on reorder) should be category-specific. **Domain context:** seasonality and promotions can distort `avg_demand`—use the same forecast version operations trusts. **Splunk:** `join` is subsearch-limited; for large catalogs, use `lookup` from a pre-built KV or summary index.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• DB Connect (or batch CSV) jobs that emit `erp:inventory_snapshot` with `material_id`, `qty_on_hand`, `plant` if used, and vendor `lead_time_days` in calendar days.
• A demand feed `forecast:demand` with `daily_demand` in the same UoM as inventory; agree whether peaks or averages drive safety stock.
• Master data owner for material status (active, obsolete) to filter noise.

Create search
• Build the subsearch as a saved report `demand_by_material_daily` if row counts exceed subsearch limits; replace the inline `search` with `| lookup demand_by_material_summary material_id OUTPUT avg_demand peak_demand` when ready.
• Run the full SPL in Search over a recent day boundary; save as `inventory_mismatch_watch`.
• Optional `eval` branch: cap `days_of_supply` display when `avg_demand` is null to avoid confusing planners.

Understanding this SPL
Inventory events are keyed by `material_id`, then a left join brings average and peak demand. `days_of_supply` divides on-hand by average demand; a large sentinel applies when demand is zero. `reorder_point` illustrates a simple safety buffer on lead time. `risk` classifies stock-out vs excess vs healthy, and the `where` drops healthy rows for action lists.

Validate
• Pick five materials: compare `qty_on_hand` to the ERP screen and `avg_demand` to the planning workbook for the same forecast version.
• Confirm `lead_time_days` matches the active approved vendor record, not a legacy route.
• During month-end freeze, verify no duplicate snapshots double-count on-hand.

Operationalize
• Daily schedule with CSV or ticket output to buyers; include `reorder_point` and `peak_demand` columns in the alert table for context.
• Pair with a dashboard filtered by plant and ABC class; document when to ignore NPI materials until twelve weeks of history exist.
• Troubleshooting: if join returns null demand, check sourcetype filters and time range on the subsearch; if everything shows excess, verify UoM conversion on the demand side.

## SPL

```spl
index=inventory sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot
| join type=left material_id [
    search index=demand sourcetype=forecast:demand
    | stats avg(daily_demand) as avg_demand, max(daily_demand) as peak_demand by material_id
  ]
| eval days_of_supply=if(avg_demand>0, round(qty_on_hand/avg_demand, 1), 9999),
       reorder_point=round(avg_demand*lead_time_days*1.2, 0)
| eval risk=case(days_of_supply<lead_time_days, "stockout_risk", days_of_supply>lead_time_days*4, "excess", 1=1, "healthy")
| where risk!="healthy"
| sort risk, days_of_supply
```

## Visualization

Scatter plot (days_of_supply vs lead_time_days, color by risk), Table (material_id, qty_on_hand, avg_demand, days_of_supply, risk), Column chart (count of at-risk materials by plant or buyer), Single value (materials in stockout_risk).

## Known False Positives

New product introductions with zero or lumpy historical demand make `avg_demand` tiny or volatile, inflating days-of-supply or falsely flagging stock-out. Maintenance shutdowns and campaign builds create temporary demand spikes that look like chronic mismatch. Multi-plant inventory without plant-level keys can merge stock that is not actually fungible. Standard cost rollouts and unit-of-measure fixes can change `qty_on_hand` without a true physical change.
